title: How to Build Multiple Businesses While Homeschooling: Heather Gerber's Blueprint
guestBio: Heather Gerber is a serial entrepreneur, homeschooling mom, and founder of Guiding Our Days, an educational program supporting public charter independent study students. She successfully runs multiple businesses including her long-time tanning business while creating meaningful community impact through education.
excerpt: Serial entrepreneur Heather Gerber proves women don't have to choose between building businesses, nurturing families, and creating community impact. Discover how she built Guiding Our Days while running her tanning business and homeschooling her children.
metaDescription: Learn how entrepreneur Heather Gerber built multiple businesses while homeschooling, creating Guiding Our Days to transform alternative education.
keyTakeaways: Women can successfully run multiple businesses without having to choose one path over another.
Community connection matters as much as curriculum when creating educational programs.
Leading with service and heart creates more sustainable and meaningful businesses.
Alternative education models can provide the flexibility modern families desperately need.
Building values-driven businesses allows you to make impact while generating income.
Entrepreneurship and motherhood can enhance each other rather than compete.
Creating educational spaces that support both kids and parents fills a crucial market gap.

pullQuotes.text: Women can build multiple businesses, nurture their families, and create meaningful community impact — all without having to choose one path over another

timestamps.label: The birth of Guiding Our Days

content:
What if I told you that the choice between building a successful business and being present for your family is a false dilemma? That you don't have to sacrifice your entrepreneurial dreams on the altar of motherhood, or vice versa? Heather Gerber's story is living proof that women can create multiple thriving businesses while homeschooling their children and building meaningful community impact.

In this powerful conversation, Heather shares how she transformed her frustration with traditional education into Guiding Our Days, an innovative program supporting public charter independent study students. But here's what makes her story even more remarkable: she didn't abandon her successful tanning business to pursue this new venture. Instead, she proves that women can hold multiple lanes of success simultaneously.

The Power of Leading with Heart

Heather's approach to business isn't just about profit margins and growth metrics. She leads with heart, recognizing that education is fundamentally about human connection. When she saw families struggling within rigid educational systems, she didn't just complain about the problem—she became the solution.

Guiding Our Days isn't just another tutoring service. It's a comprehensive support system that recognizes what many educational institutions miss: community matters as much as curriculum. Heather understands that when you're serving families, you're not just delivering academic content—you're creating a space where both kids and parents feel supported, understood, and empowered.

This heart-centered approach extends beyond her educational venture. Running her tanning business with the same values-driven mindset, Heather demonstrates that integrity and authenticity aren't just nice-to-haves in business—they're essential ingredients for sustainable success.

Redefining What Success Looks Like

Society often pressures women to choose: be a dedicated mother or a serious entrepreneur, focus on one business or spread yourself too thin, prioritize family or chase professional success. Heather's story dismantles these false choices entirely. She shows us that success doesn't have to look like the narrow definition we've been sold.

Running multiple businesses while homeschooling isn't about having superhuman abilities or perfect time management. It's about alignment. When your businesses reflect your values and support your family's lifestyle, they become integrated parts of your life rather than competing priorities.

Heather's journey reveals that the real magic happens when you stop trying to fit into someone else's blueprint for success and start creating your own. Her businesses work together, supporting her family's needs while allowing her to make a meaningful impact in her community.

Building Businesses That Serve

The most striking aspect of Heather's approach is how she's built businesses that serve a purpose beyond profit. Guiding Our Days emerged from a genuine need she observed in her community—families craving flexibility and connection in their educational journey. This service-first mindset creates businesses that are not only financially sustainable but personally fulfilling.

When you build a business around solving real problems for real people, you tap into something more powerful than traditional marketing strategies. You create genuine demand because you're meeting genuine needs. Heather's educational program thrives because it addresses the pain points that traditional systems often ignore or dismiss.

This approach also creates businesses with staying power. When your work is rooted in service and aligned with your values, you're less likely to burn out or lose passion for what you're building. You're not just chasing external metrics of success—you're creating something that feeds your soul while serving others.

The Future of Flexible Work and Education

Heather's story points toward a future that many of us are hungry for: flexible, family-centered approaches to both work and education. The traditional model of compartmentalized life—work here, family there, personal interests somewhere else—is increasingly obsolete.

The families choosing alternative education models like Guiding Our Days aren't rejecting structure—they're seeking structures that work for their unique situations. Similarly, women building portfolio careers aren't being indecisive—they're being strategic about creating lives that honor all aspects of who they are.

This shift requires us to think differently about leadership, success, and impact. It means recognizing that a homeschooling mom who builds multiple businesses isn't doing too much—she's modeling what integrated living can look like. She's showing other women that they don't have to shrink their dreams to fit their circumstances.

Permission to Dream Bigger

If you've been telling yourself that you need to choose between your various dreams and responsibilities, Heather's story gives you permission to reconsider. Maybe the problem isn't that you want too much—maybe it's that you've been thinking too small about what's possible.

Building a life and business that fits your family, your values, and your vision isn't just a nice idea—it's an achievable reality. It requires courage to step outside conventional models and wisdom to create systems that support your unique goals. But most importantly, it requires you to believe that you deserve a life where you don't have to choose between the things that matter most to you.

Heather Gerber's journey reminds us that women are capable of extraordinary things when they lead with heart, build with purpose, and refuse to accept false limitations. The question isn't whether you can have it all—it's whether you're ready to define what all means for you.

guestLinks.label: 🔗 Learn more about Guiding Our Days
📚 Grab your copy of The Manage Her

quiz.title: What's Your Multi-Lane Success Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach building multiple dreams simultaneously and what that reveals about your unique path to success.

questions.question: When you have a new business idea, what's your first instinct?

options.text: I immediately start thinking about how this could solve a real problem in the world
I research the market and create a detailed plan before taking any action
I reach out to my network to get feedback and see who might want to collaborate
I consider how this fits with my existing commitments and life goals

questions.question: How do you typically handle having multiple interests or projects going at once?

options.text: I follow my passion and intuition, trusting that the right opportunities will align
I prioritize them based on potential return and allocate my time accordingly
I look for ways to connect them and create synergy between different projects
I try to find natural rhythms and seasons for different focuses

questions.question: What drives you most when building something new?

options.text: The desire to make a meaningful impact that aligns with my values
The satisfaction of creating something sustainable and well-structured
The opportunity to bring people together and build community
The chance to create more freedom and flexibility in my life

questions.question: When facing a major decision about your business or career, you tend to:

options.text: Trust your gut feeling about what feels right and purposeful
Analyze the data and potential outcomes before deciding
Talk it through with trusted friends, mentors, or family members
Consider how each option affects all areas of your life

questions.question: What's your biggest concern about pursuing multiple business ventures?

options.text: Losing sight of my core mission and values in the pursuit of growth
Not having enough systems in place to manage everything effectively
Not being able to give each community or client the attention they deserve
Creating more chaos instead of the balanced life I'm seeking

questions.question: How do you measure success in your ventures?

options.text: By the positive impact I'm making and how aligned I feel with my purpose
By consistent growth, profitability, and the strength of my business foundation
By the relationships I've built and the community I've created
By how well everything works together to support the life I want to live

results.title: The Heart-Led Visionary
results.description: You see opportunities everywhere and naturally connect the dots between different passions and purposes. Like Heather, you believe that leading with heart isn't just nice—it's essential for sustainable success. You understand that impact and income can grow together when you stay true to your values. This episode will inspire you to embrace all your entrepreneurial dreams without apology and show you exactly how someone else is thriving in multiple lanes while staying grounded in what matters most.

results.title: The Strategic Builder
results.description: You're naturally drawn to creating systems and structures that support long-term growth. You understand that sustainable success requires intention and planning, and you're not afraid to take a methodical approach to building multiple ventures. You value both stability and expansion, seeing them as complementary rather than competing forces. Heather's blueprint for managing multiple businesses while homeschooling will give you concrete strategies for scaling your impact without sacrificing the foundation you've worked so hard to build.

results.title: The Community Connector
results.description: You instinctively understand that success is sweeter when shared and that building meaningful connections is just as important as building profitable businesses. Like Heather discovered with her educational program, you know that community matters as much as content in whatever you create. You're naturally drawn to ventures that bring people together and solve real problems. This episode will validate your belief that relationships are your greatest business asset and show you how to monetize your gift for creating belonging.

results.title: The Balanced Integrator
results.description: You refuse to accept the false choice between different aspects of your life and are constantly seeking ways to make everything work together harmoniously. You believe that entrepreneurship and motherhood can enhance each other rather than compete, and you're always looking for creative solutions that honor all parts of who you are. Heather's story will resonate deeply with you and provide the real-world proof that integration isn't just possible—it's powerful.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manage Her, the podcast where we illuminate the invisible work women do every day and show how it translates into powerful real world leadership. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author, entrepreneur, mama of six, and passionate believer that what we do at home is just as valuable as what we do in business.

Today's guest is someone who's making a difference in both. Heather Gerber is the founder of Guiding Our Days, a unique educational program that supports public charter independent study students with tutoring services and enrichment opportunities. It's a lifeline for so many families navigating alternative education. And trust me, I say that from experience. Two of my children attend Heather's program, and it's located right in our same business park. It's made homeschooling and working not only possible, but joyful.

Heather is also a serial entrepreneur who previously ran a successful tanning business for 15 years. She knows what it takes to build something from the ground up, and she's turned her passion for service and community into a safe, nurturing, and empowering space for kids and parents alike. Today we'll talk about what it takes to create values-driven business, how Heather balances motherhood and entrepreneurship, and why the future of education might look a lot more like community than classrooms. Let's get into it.

Hi, Heather. Thanks for being here today.

**Heather:** So excited to be here.

**Aimee:** Oh, so happy to have you. You really are quite the entrepreneur. We were just talking about how you're still running your tanning business.

**Heather:** I am. It's not like it was in the beginning.

**Aimee:** Awesome, though. But it's a great little side hustle that supplements your life.

**Heather:** It does. I used to have a storefront, but that door is closed and then a different one's opened, but it runs out of my home now.

**Aimee:** That's amazing.

**Heather:** My husband built out my garage, so I could have dinner cooking, laundry going, spray tanning - all the things at once.

**Aimee:** That's awesome. And I also didn't know that you were also a dental hygienist.

**Heather:** Yes. That was for 20 years. That ended just right after COVID.

**Aimee:** Nice. It changed dentistry.

**Heather:** It really did significantly. It really did.

**Aimee:** Well, I'm just so happy to have my kids in a place where they're safe and they're learning and Indie could be at her level because in traditional school, a three-year-old can't go to TK.

**Heather:** No, they can't. They're not.

**Aimee:** I can't believe she was three when she started, by the way.

**Heather:** She was three when she started.

**Aimee:** She's not really three, though.

**Heather:** I know. She turned four in November. So to see her blossom this year - she is reading and writing and drawing and loves it and math and all the things. In traditional school system I wouldn't have been able to give her that kind of experience.

**Aimee:** True. They would have been held back to small group too if she was in such a large group. She has such a big light to shine.

**Heather:** She gets to shine so bright in the small little learning environment.

**Aimee:** It's so true. She's everybody's favorite. She has a fan following, I have to say.

**Heather:** I know. She's a character, that one. She is something else.

**Aimee:** I mean, for a child to be so excited about everything, even coming to pick up the dirty mess. She's like, "Okay, let's do it." Please rub off on more. She's very - I think enthusiasm has always been my superpower, and I think she may have also picked up enthusiasm as one of her superpowers.

**Heather:** I would definitely give you credit for that.

**Aimee:** She's a doll. Well, we're very grateful that there are - Daniel though, too. My Daniel is also there. Just finished fifth grade. Finishing up fifth grade. And he is just - well, he's a real smart kid.

**Heather:** He is, but he's more quiet and reserved.

**Aimee:** Yes. But let me tell you, he has a gift for public speaking.

**Heather:** He turns on real quick. I am beyond impressed with him. He's blown all his classmates out of the water, too, where they're like, "Hey, Daniel, you are so good at that. You're so natural." And he's so humble about it.

**Aimee:** He's so cute. He basically was in TK when COVID hit.

**Heather:** So, he jumped, right? He actually skipped most of kindergarten because he had already learned to read.

**Aimee:** Okay.

**Heather:** And so, he was doing a little program. One of our friends was running a little kindergarten out of our house and he goes, "Mom, I don't want to be with the babies anymore. I can read. I can read," he tells me. And I go, "Okay, prove it." And so he shows me he can read. And I said, "Okay, the beginning of next semester, you're coming in, you'll be in first grade."

And so he was in class. He was the youngest kid in class. And we went from first grade to sixth grade. And those kids had to do presentations. We did presentations on Pompeii. We did - they had to memorize passages of the Declaration of Independence and present it to us. Presentations truly are worth far more than just reading a book and writing down answers.

**Aimee:** I mean, yeah. And I think because he jumped into that from such a young age and watching kids perform at a much higher level, he's always been really at a higher level when it comes to reading, when it comes to his writing. I love the way he writes.

**Heather:** I agree.

**Aimee:** His writing is so awesome. And he has a lot to share with his classmates but he's undercover a little bit and that's what I love about him.

**Heather:** He is undercover. He is very quiet. He's so the very middle child and so classic.

**Aimee:** Yeah, he really is. He just flies under the radar all the time. I always forget about him every occasion. I'm really proud of him.

**Heather:** He's an amazing human. He really really is. I've been trying to just give him extra love. As you become more aware you're like oh that kid needs extra love cuz he's right there.

**Aimee:** Because he's not going to demand that. No, he doesn't. He does not do anything. I can tell. I can see that.

**Heather:** No, he just will hang back and get whatever there's left over and it's so sad.

**Aimee:** I know. I love your kids so much, so I know we're here talking about other things, but I'm like, wait, we have to mention Daniel, too.

But as a serial entrepreneur and a mom, how do you balance your personal and professional identities over the years?

**Heather:** Oh, it's a dance. It's a dance.

**Aimee:** Yeah. It's not always pretty.

**Heather:** Sometimes I fantasized about, oh, what would it be like to just be just an entrepreneur or just a mom, right? I don't know. But doing just one might be too easy. I don't know. Cuz anything that is worth it in life is hard. It's challenging. It will stretch you. It'll grow you.

But I first say my identity comes from the Lord personally, not from my profession, which I think is hard for a lot of people. They identify themselves personally from their profession, right? And so that's been important for me to remember that my identity comes from the Lord first and that I can have these different professions.

**Aimee:** Yeah, absolutely.

**Heather:** But to manage it all. So what was the question again? I can go in so many different directions.

**Aimee:** No, just about balancing yourself. Balancing motherhood.

**Heather:** I'm always balanced.

**Aimee:** But I sure try because I think as moms we're trying to be perfect all the time and do it all. And so giving ourselves that grace to not have it be perfect all the time. And sometimes there's been motherhood that's taken just the primary seat or sometimes my business. It's not always been balanced perfectly. It's a dance. You're right. It's more of a dance. It's not a balance. It's more about finding a rhythm. We say it's more about a rhythm.

**Aimee:** Exactly. Yeah. Totally. Yeah. And sometimes it's easier and sometimes it's harder.

**Heather:** Yes. Yes. Most definitely.

**Aimee:** Depending on the phases that your kids and your businesses are going through.

**Heather:** Definitely. And how many businesses you're doing. That can add to it.

**Aimee:** Definitely. And you're doing so many.

**Heather:** I know. But I love that I've experienced being an employee and an owner as well because there's a lot to learn as being an employee, too.

**Aimee:** Definitely.

**Heather:** And watching how other employers run their businesses, too.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I've had many conversations where it's like, if you really want to get good at something, go get a job, right? And go learn from a really good company. A big company will give you training.

**Heather:** It's so true. And Brendan, my husband always said, he wouldn't have been as far along in soft skills and sales had he not worked for the St. Regis because that the five-star training, the customer service training that he got in his 20s. There's a lot of value in that.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Heather:** Well, they've got the money to figure out how to train people to do stuff. Let them train you. Get the education.

**Aimee:** I know. I did that with so many dentists that I worked for. They'd spend so much money sending us to different trainings. And I've been able to take bits and pieces from all of that throughout the years.

**Heather:** Right? Yeah. If you're an employee, make sure you're getting the value out of it, getting the education that's going to be available to you as an employee. Smart. Love that.

**Aimee:** So cool.

**Heather:** And learn from people's mistakes.

**Aimee:** Yes, please. And your own - learn from your own, too. I mean, we do naturally. When you get to learn through someone else, oh, definitely. Yeah. You don't have to go through it necessarily yourself.

**Heather:** Yeah. When you're working at other dental offices, you got to see what works. I'm looking and going, "Oh, okay." Note to self.

**Aimee:** Note to self. Maybe don't - maybe we don't do that.

**Heather:** Yeah. Exactly. Maybe we do a little more of that. I don't know.

**Aimee:** So, so many women think that they have to choose between motherhood and entrepreneurship. What has been your experience that has taught you about managing both?

**Heather:** I know. I think that's a lie that we have to choose between both. Again, it comes back to also feeling like we have to do everything so perfectly. But I do think that they can complement each other, that they don't have to compete. And a lot of times I find it intentional.

**Aimee:** Yeah. You got to be intentional about it. But sometimes when you go in with the intention of this entrepreneurial project, this side hustle is going to give me more time to be with my kids.

**Heather:** Yes. And I've learned sometimes that's actually the opposite, too.

**Aimee:** Right.

**Heather:** So, what I've learned is that I need to create my business around my values.

**Aimee:** Let's talk about Guiding Our Days. What inspired you to start the program and how does it serve families in a way traditional schools might not?

**Heather:** My spray tanning business was doing great and I could have never imagined being in education. First of all, I think I've told you before that if someone told me that that's what I'd be doing, I'd be laughing. I would laugh and just say, "Oh, sure."

But my husband was diagnosed with cancer in 2015 and I was running the tanning business, dental hygienist, and the whole world just changed completely because he had a major surgery and then he went through chemo for a year and a half and was bedridden for almost 3 years.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Heather:** But with that change, I realized my time with my family is more important than ever. And I didn't want to be tied to a dental chair. I don't want to be tied to spray tanning at a certain time. So, I got into direct sales to kind of scale back to be there for my husband's chemo appointments, take care of him. Had a toddler that was three years old at the time. I still was tanning and doing different things, but the shift happened there.

And then when my youngest daughter was 2 years old, she had a vaccine injury.

**Aimee:** Oh.

**Heather:** And that's when everything was really crystal clear, concrete that we're going to be a homeschooling family. I would never give them another vaccine. My oldest daughter didn't have serious complications, but dyslexia, ADHD - there's a lot of different things. Some people believe that that is a thing and some people don't and that's okay. But that was what I experienced and I knew that okay, I have to pivot. We're not going to be a public or private school family. So I was committed to teaching my own children, not a whole bunch of other children. But that is really kind of how that started.

**Aimee:** Interesting. So SB276 affected you. SB277, SB276. These were laws in California that changed - basically made it so that we would have to have - first it was 277 and it was that one took away the personal belief exemption and the religious exemption from vaccination. But they said they would leave our medical exemptions. But 2 years later in 2019, they came for our medical exemptions and now children like ours who are medically fragile, you have to choose to potentially injure your child if you want your child to attend public or private school in the state of California.

**Heather:** And so there are a lot of us that have switched into homeschooling because it is -

**Aimee:** Move out of state.

**Heather:** Or move out of state. Those are your options. Exactly.

**Aimee:** Yeah. If you're not willing to put your child in danger, which most moms aren't.

**Heather:** I mean I feel so sad for the moms that don't have a choice that didn't figure out a way that had to work or felt that they didn't have a choice.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Heather:** But I think for all of us who were more creative and actually just big huge giant mama bears. So I didn't realize that you were in the same boat. Everything was on track and at 2 years she had her DTaP vaccine and that day she stopped making eye contact and stopped speaking. She just - I mean I could say her name she just would look right past me and she didn't speak for 2 years. I mean if you met her now no one would ever know but thank goodness to heavy metal detoxing and speech therapy and just a lot of intentional care. She's thriving and doing great. But that is - it's not exactly how Guiding Our Days started, but it was a big shift into that direction.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Because here you are now you're homeschooling and - yeah. Now by 2020 you've got - I mean now I think there are something like 50,000 children in the state of California that are basically barred from attending public and private school.

**Heather:** I mean most of the children at my program have something along those lines.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. So, you're serving an underserved community, but a community there's a need.

**Heather:** Yeah. And there's still so many people that don't know that there's people like me out there that they didn't have that choice. I remember last summer a mom just cried when she met me. She's like, I thought I didn't have any other choice. And that makes me sad to think that we've come so far in the homeschooling world and that still there's moms that don't know about it.

**Aimee:** Yeah, it's true. Yes, you can homeschool your kids. But if you work full-time, then you think, well, how is that possible?

**Heather:** Right.

**Aimee:** But it's by finding programs like these. There are programs out there in your community. Multiple - I mean, we have multiple programs in this area growing and I'm so excited.

**Heather:** I think it's great.

**Aimee:** Everyone needs a home and all these programs can serve. Yeah. There's an abundance of people and families that are in need of this type of program.

**Heather:** Well, and then the money is the other factor. So, moms need to work. They're like, "Well, how am I going to homeschool or how do I pay for such an expensive program? Maybe they can't afford that." But then it's like, "Oh, but you can use your charter funds."

**Aimee:** So, we are allowed - basically we can tap in to some of the funds that our children would have had access to had they been able to go to public school. Correct. It's not as much.

**Heather:** Oh, no. They're keeping a good - yeah, cuz I believe we pay in taxes $13,000, $13-15,000 that we should be getting. But we get about $4,000 a year.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Where does it all go? I don't -

**Heather:** I don't know. I know. I know they've been trying to push a bill through in Sacramento that would give us the full amount of money because it really would if we had the full amount of money to school our children. It would be life-changing because we're all supplementing our funds to get our kids educated.

**Aimee:** And we're making progress.

**Heather:** Yeah, we are. We are making progress. Definitely. And more parents are learning about it.

And so when I really had to close the door on dental hygiene and what really pushed me into Guiding Our Days was I had a car accident that debilitated me and I lost all mobility in my hands and my arms and was in so much nerve pain. And I just really sought after the Lord more than ever and I said use this pain teach me grow me sharpen me bring me closer to you but more than anything I want to know what your purpose is for me. I want to do what you have called for me because I lost tanning, dental, and direct sales. I couldn't even hold a phone.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Heather:** I would tell people those big old hands that you'd see at the baseball games, those foam hands, that's how big my hands felt. I couldn't even use them for anything. Yeah. And so I'm like, just use me for what your purpose is. And I went to this church service and this woman grabbed me and said, "Oh my gosh, I'm supposed to pray for you." She prayed over me. I was radically healed. I went to that service - no makeup, hair matted. I couldn't even brush my own hair. And I left that service and I was whole. I was transformed in my mind, my body, and it was crystal clear. He said, "You're going to build my kingdom, and it starts with the children." And I didn't know what that meant at the time, but he just gave me enough pieces and guided me every step of the way. And it's a longer story than that, but here we are today.

**Aimee:** Wow. I love that.

**Heather:** So, I wake up every day and pinch myself in awe of how did I get from there to here? And I just am so fulfilled getting to be with these children. Oh my gosh. And I know this is where I'm supposed to be.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Well, you have such a special program and such special children there. And it's so nice because it is such a small program and that's this intimate educational ecosystem where the big kids and the little kids and the teachers are so sweet and it's just providing for all of these - they're able to get these kids and have such one-on-one.

**Heather:** It is very much one-on-one. Yeah.

**Aimee:** Literally Miss Nicole with Indie, she does one-on-one multiple times throughout the day, which I don't know how many programs get to do that.

**Heather:** No, it's really kids in a group. That's still pretty intimate, but she does one on one. But what I love about the siblings like Daniel and Indie for instance on the field - there's Indie being with the older boys playing football and then Indie'll go give her a moment and push her on the swings. And it's just there's so many siblings in there from TK all the way to 8th grade. It's just one big family. Big brothers, little sisters.

**Aimee:** It's way more tribal. It feels more like how we were supposed to be doing this, how we're supposed to be raising our children or how it was back in the day when you could put your kids out on the street. I know in your neighborhood you can't in my neighborhood. I don't know about my neighborhood, but yeah. How it was when we were growing up.

**Heather:** Yeah. Yeah. It's really, really sweet. It's very special.

**Aimee:** What makes the model unique and why do you think that this is growing in popularity? Cuz it really is.

**Heather:** I think people are waking up and realizing that maybe these school systems don't have the best intentions for our children. And that there's other options. So, I think parents are waking up to that and it really empowers the parent to be the primary influence in their child's life.

You send your kid to school, I mean really anywhere 5 days a week, it's not a bad thing, but we don't know these people. We're entrusting them to instill values, what they're influenced by. It's a lot that we're just kind of handing over and trusting.

**Aimee:** Yes.

**Heather:** And so, we have that power to choose who their teachers are, what children and families they're surrounded by.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Yeah. It's pretty amazing. Yeah, I can see - be in the driver's seat.

**Heather:** I can see that. Yeah, I know. That's one of the things that we - Someone was talking about it yesterday that the public schools are not growing that they're actually shrinking.

**Aimee:** They really are. They're shrinking and changing their models. I heard some that are going to be doing three days a week and two days at home. And that's pretty big for the public schools that they're having to do that.

**Heather:** Very big. They're having - yeah. It's basically forcing them to shut down. Yeah. Because now people are demanding a different type of learning styles.

**Aimee:** They're like, "Hey, why aren't you guys doing - why can't we have that?"

**Heather:** Yeah. Yeah. Totally. Because you can learn what you need to learn in three days. We do it.

**Aimee:** We sure do. Yeah. Because there's a lot more to learn just in a classroom.

**Heather:** Yeah.

**Aimee:** I want my kids to know how to do some laundry, too.
